data.title: Merck's Deal For Terns And Its Stellar CML Drug Candidate Looks Like Good Business
data.description:
Investment Overview

Today, news has broken that Terns Pharmaceuticals, Inc. (TERN), a Foster City, California-based biotech, is set to be acquired by the Pharma giant Merck & Co., Inc. (MRK), in a deal worth $53 per share, or ~$6.7bn - according to a press release issued today:

This equates to approximately $5.7 billion net of acquired cash and represents an approximate premium of 31% to the 60-day and 42% to the 90-day volume-weighted average stock price on March 24, 2026.

The release also states:

Terns’ lead candidate, TERN-701, is a novel investigational oral allosteric BCR::ABL1 tyrosine kinase inhibitor (TKI) currently being evaluated in the Phase 1/2 CARDINAL trial (NCT06163430) for patients with Philadelphia chromosome-positive (Ph+), chronic phase chronic myeloid leukemia (CML) previously treated with at least one prior TKI and who experienced treatment failure, suboptimal response or treatment intolerance. In March 2024, the U.S. Food and Drug Administration (FDA) granted Orphan Drug Designation for TERN-701 for the treatment of CML.

Analysis - Terns' Phenomenal Rise From The Ashes

Terns has experienced a phenomenal rise in its share price across the past 12 months - its stock is now up a scarcely believable >1,400% during this period, in fact. I gave TERN stock a Buy rating when it traded at $4.9 per share in July last year, discussing its two clinical stage drug candidates as follows:

The first is TERN-701, which is indicated for chronic myeloid leukemia ("CML")—a "$5bn market, Tern's research indicates. This drug targets the harder-to-reach "allosteric," as opposed to orthosteric sites of proteins, and it is a tyrosine kinase inhibitor ("TKI"). Terns compares its potency favorably to asciminib, marketed and sold by Swiss Pharma giant Novartis (NVS) as Scemblix, earning $689m of revenues in 2024.

There are 15 patients enrolled in a Phase 1 study of TERN-701, and the "major molecular response" rate, a key efficacy measure used in evaluating CML patients response to treatment, has been "highly encouraging," management says. Additional Phase 1 data is expected in Q4.

The second candidate is TERN-601, an oral glucagon-like peptide ("GLP-1") agonist, designed with GI tolerability in mind.

Terns shelved development of its GLP-1 asset in October last year in October last year, after underwhelming Phase 2 data, but it scarcely mattered, because on November 3rd 2025 the company blew the markets expectations away with data from its Phase 1 study in r/r CML. According to a press release:

Of 32 efficacy-evaluable patients:

  • Overall (cumulative) major molecular response (MMR) rate of 75% (24/32) by 24 weeks, with 64% (14/22) achieving MMR and 100% (10/10) maintaining MMR
  • Overall (cumulative) MMR by 24 weeks in difficult to treat patient subgroups:
    • 69% (11/16) in patients with lack of efficacy to last tyrosine kinase inhibitor (TKI)
    • 60% (6/10) in patients who had prior asciminib
    • 67% (8/12) in patients with prior asciminib / ponatinib / investigational TKI
  • No patients had lost MMR at the time of data cutoff.

Amy Burroughs, CEO of Terns, said that the 24 weeks MMR achievement rate was:

unprecedented, trending at least two times higher than the rates reported in other Phase 1 studies of CML therapies that are approved or in development. Importantly, TERN-701 also achieved consistently high overall (cumulative) MMR rates in key, difficult to treat patient subgroups while maintaining an encouraging safety profile.

By mid-December, stock had reached $47 per share. The mechanism of action of TERN-601, being an allosteric inhibitor, is similar to that of Swiss Pharma giant Novartis' (NVS) Scemblix (asciminib), approved to treat CML in 2021, and earning ~$1.3bn of revenues in 2025, up >80% year-on-year.

In its 2025 annual report / 10-K filing, Terns notes that:

Allosteric TKIs are highly selective to the ABL1 myristoyl-binding pocket, and virtually inactive against other cellular kinases, avoiding the off-target effects of the active-site TKIs. Furthermore, mutations in the active-site pocket, such as T315I, may occur frequently, which may ultimately render active-site TKIs ineffective against CML.

Terns suggests in a recent corporate presentation however that asciminib's efficacy, safety and convenience can be improved upon, as ~32% of first-line treated patients do not reach MMR at 48 weeks, ~57% of second line do not reach it at 24 weeks, and ~75% of third line patients do not reach it at 24 weeks. Pancreatic toxicity and hypertension are also common side effects, Terns says.

As we can see above, Terns points to its 75% MMR achievement at its 320mg dose (asciminib achieved a 24% response in a Phase 1 clinical study, and 25.5% in a Phase 3 study, Terns says), deep molecular response ("DMR") rate of 36%, lack of dose-limiting toxicities, low instances of serious adverse events, and once-per-day dosing as potential competitive advantages.

As I concluded in a December 2025 note on Terns (upholding my Buy rating):

While there are always holes to pick in any clinical study readout, the robustness of Terns' data is notable - seemingly, TERN-701 beats Scemblix all ends up - there are multiple examples of outperformance across different patient profiles, types of response, and prior treatment regimes, all of them pointing to TERN-701's potential superiority.

The bull case is therefore simple in many ways - repeat its outstanding performance in its Phase 1/2 CARDINAL study in its Phase 3, secure an approval based on that data, and launch as a best-in-class therapy in CML, rapidly eating into Scemblix, the current standard of care's market share.

Analysis - Why Merck? Why Now?

Merck is a global Pharma powerhouse, headquartered in New Jersey. In 2025, the company posted revenues of $65bn, net income of $18.25bn, and earnings per share ("EPS") of $7.3. For 2026, the company is guiding for $65.5bn - $67bn of revenues, and non-GAAP EPS of $5 - $5.15, reflecting a one-time charge of ~$3.65 per share related to its $9.2bn acquisition of Cidara Therapeutics and its antiviral influenza candidate.

In 2025, Merck's oncology division earned $35bn, or ~53% of its total revenues, its vaccines division $10.5bn, or ~16% of revenues, cardiovascular division $2bn, hospital acute care $3.3bn, virology $1.3bn, Diabetes $2.73bn, and Animal Health $6.5bn, or 10% of revenues.

As such, we may conclude oncology is Merck's key strength, however $31bn of its 2025 revenues were driven by a single asset, the immune checkpoint inhibitor (ICI) targeting PD-1, Keytruda, which is approved to treat multiple solid tumor cancers.

Keytruda is set to lose key patent patent protections in the U.S. around the end of 2028, and will likely face inflation reduction act ("IRA") price controls in that year also, resulting in rapidly falling revenues.

In summary, Merck is under pressure to find new sources of long-term revenues from patent protected prescription drugs, which drive high profit margins - a reward for the billions of dollars of R&D costs associated with developing new drug candidates.

As opposed to developing such assets, Pharmas like Merck also focus on strategic M&A deals to bolster their product portfolio or pipeline, and in that respect, Merck has been busy. Besides acquiring Cidara, the Pharma also completed the ~$10bn acquisition of Verona Pharma and its chronic obstructive pulmonary disease ("COPD") therapy Ohtuvayre in July last year.

On a call with analysts to discuss its proposed deal for Terns today, Merck's President and CEO Robert Davis told analysts:

We are transforming our company with launches underway for more than 20 anticipated new growth drivers, almost all of which have blockbuster potential and together represent a combined non-risk-adjusted potential commercial opportunity of over $70 billion by the mid-2030s.

Innovation-driven value-enhancing business development is an important element of our strategy to expand and diversify our pipeline across a broad set of therapeutic areas, including oncology.

In relation to TERBN-701, they also commented:

challenges with today's treatments related to durability and tolerability lead to frequent switching. TERN-701 is designed to address these unmet needs and provide an important potential new option for patients. Given the size of the addressable population and the remaining unmet medical need, we believe TERN-701 has multibillion-dollar commercial potential and will be a significant driver of growth in the next decade.

Meanwhile, Merck's President of Merck Research Laboratories Dean Li told analysts:

We reviewed the totality of data for TERN-701 and estimate that the final MMR achievement using a more conservative intent-to-treat population would be approximately double the MMR and double to triple the DMR achievement of approved TKIs, including Asciminib.

Merck's current oncology division only actually contains a handful of assets - Keytruda, a subcutaneous version of Keytruda (helping to extend patent protections in several key indications beyond 2028, potentially), heme cancer med Welireg, $739m revenues last year, and alliance revenues pertaining to Lynparza (ovarian cancer), Lenvima (thyroid cancer) and Reblozyl (myelodyspalstic syndromes).

Its pipeline contains >10 potentially approvable assets, however, and Li noted in today's call that:

we have a series of pivotal readouts from a pipeline of innovative hematology candidates within the next couple of years, including Nemtabrutinib, our non-covalent BTK inhibitor, Bomedemstat, our LSD1 inhibitor and Zilovertamab vedotin, our ROR1 targeting ADC. As well as MK-1045, our CD19xCD3 T-cell engager, which is being evaluated in a Phase I/II study.

My modelling suggests these assets could contribute up to ~$20bn to Merck's top line by 2030, although that is a ballpark estimate. As of the end of 2025, Merck reported a cash position of $14.6bn, and a long-term debt position of $46.75bn, which sounds onerous, although we may note that S&P Global recently assigned a proposed issue of multi-tranche senior unsecured notes as A+, noting that:

Our ‘A+’ issuer credit rating reflects the company’s leading market position in oncology with Keytruda and its broad product portfolio including vaccines and animal health products. The company is diversifying its pipeline and portfolio ahead of an expected Keytruda sales decline in 2028 due to patent expiration. It is achieving this through increased focus on R&D, as well as the acquisitions of Verona Pharma and Cidara Therapeutics. We expect the company’s S&P Global Ratings-adjusted net leverage to generally remain below our 2x downside threshold for the rating.

In summary, then, Merck has been in the market for strategic M&A deals, even despite its impressive array of pipeline assets (which extend to vaccines, cardiovascular, and immunology also), and in Terns, its research has clearly concluded that TERN-701 is a multi "Blockbuster" (>$1bn revenues per annum) in waiting.

Risks To Merck's Deal For Terns

Merck says it intends to finance its latest deal "primarily through new debt, and we do not expect an impact to our credit rating." That may be true, but Merck's M&A funds are surely not unlimited, and therefore by buying Terns, it may miss out on other strategic deals which may present themselves over the course of 2026 and beyond.

The deal will also impact Merck's 2026 EPS - as the company's Chief Financial Officer Caroline Litchfield put it today:

we expect a charge to research and development expense in 2026 of approximately $5.8 billion or approximately $2.35 per share. In addition, we expect a negative impact to EPS of approximately $0.17 in the first 12 months, which represents investment to advance the development of TERN-701 and the assumed cost of financing.

While Terns data to date has been undeniably strong, the drug must still negotiate its way through a pivotal study, and a regulatory approval process, which may uncover as-yet unknown issues with its mechanism of action, affecting safety or efficacy.

The slide above (taken from a Terns corporate presentation) highlights pivotal study plans, and if we consider the fact that a pivotal study is unlikely to initiate before late 2026, with a primary endpoint of MMR achievement at 48 weeks, with a second study starting later and also lasting 48 weeks, and the traditional 10-months FDA approval process, its hard to imagine Tern-701 being approved much before 2029. By that time, Scemblix could be a $2bn - $3bn selling asset with an entrenched market position.

Terns also notes in its 2025 annual report / 10-K filing:

We are aware of both pharmaceutical and biotechnology companies with development programs in CML. Companies that have recently participated in or are participating in the development of CML treatments include, but are not limited to, Ascentage Pharma Group, BristolMyers Squibb Company (BMY), Enliven Therapeutics Inc., Jiangsu Hansoh Pharmaceutical Group Company Ltd., Novartis Pharmaceuticals Corp., Pfizer Inc. (PFE), Shenzhen TargetRx, Inc., Sun Pharma Industries Ltd., and Takeda Pharmaceutical Co., Ltd (TAK).

As such, we can point to several risks to the deal - the length of time to approval, the possibility of a pivotal study setback or failure, competitive threats, and the inability to make alternative M&A deals.

Concluding Thoughts: What's The Play On Merck / Terns Today?

Personally, I would fully expect this deal to complete sometime next quarter. Terns shareholder's have realized a phenomenal return on any investment made before January this year, but both they and management will likely conclude that passing its prime asset to a Pharma giant like Merck, with its commercial experience, infrastructure, and funding, is the right move, at precisely the right time, in my view. I therefore conclude we will not see much movement in Terns share price prior to deal completion.

From Merck's perspective, its current market cap of $296bn is ~5x forward sales, and share price of $120 ~24x forward EPS, prior to any readjustments made relating to today's deal announcement. About average for the "Big Pharma" sector, while its annual dividend yield is ~3% presently.

Merck undoubtedly faces a huge challenge in relation to its Keytruda patent expiry, but with its packed pipeline and potential new drug approvals between now and 2028+ (shown below, source: earnings presentation) I would actually back the company to emerge as a stronger and more diverse business post Keytruda's loss of exclusivity ("LOE").

One Pharma company that coped admirably with the patent expiry of its best-selling asset Humira, the immunology "mega-blockbuster," is AbbVie (ABBV) - its stock is up 100% on a five-year basis, despite Humira revenues falling from >$21bn in 2022, to <$5bn in 2025.

The luxury of having a mega-blockbuster like Keytruda driving a high profit margin is that there is plenty of cash coming into the business, which can be deployed on M&A deals, although most of Humira's revenues lost revenues are in fact offset by two internally developed assets, Skyrizi and Rinvoq.

There is (in my view) more risk in buying in biotechs assets via M&A, especially ones that are unlikely to revenue generating for several years, but with Ohtuvayre already commercialized (with peak revenue expectations of >$3bn per annum), and so many other assets sharing pivotal data and pushing for approval, I'd expect a steady trickle of positive news-flow to keep shares relatively buoyant.

With that said, Merck stock has been on a tremendous recent run, rising >50% across the past six months, compared to a gain of just 65% on a five-year basis, which makes me wonder if it may be vulnerable to volatility to the downside, for example when Merck releases its Terns acquisition adjusted guidance, likely when announcing Q1 earnings around April 30th.

I gave Merck stock a Buy rating in late January, after the company pulled out of a deal to buy Revolution Medicines, potentially for a fee of >$25bn, which I felt would have added too many pre-commercial assets to an already crammed oncology pipeline.

With Terns, Merck takes on more risk, given it is buying just one asset, but for a much cheaper price, and that one asset, if it repeats its Phase 1 performance in a pivotal study, may make this deal look like a steal - I'd also speculate other Pharmas may have been in the market for Tern-701, so to me this looks like a calculated gamble with a strong chance of paying off.

As such, while I wouldn't necessarily make Merck a top Pharma pick today. If the stock takes a haircut in 2026 on downgraded guidance or a late stage study failure, for example, I would certainly think about buying MRK stock, because I continue to expect a longer-term uptrend.

data.title: Super Micro: Cheap For A Reason, And Not Cheap Enough
data.description:
Super Micro Computer, Inc. (SMCI) corrected by 25-30% following news that three individuals linked to the company, including a cofounder, were charged by US authorities with alleged violations related to export controls of server shipments to China. The company itself has not been named as a defendant. The investigation is underway, but the market reaction has been immediate and severe, with some minor recovery in the past couple of sessions.

Importantly, this sharp correction has come on top of an already weak trend, with the stock trading at nearly half the levels seen in the October peaks, coinciding with a larger market trend where AI trades have started unwinding and long-duration assumptions are being challenged. SMCI had its own share of intrinsic concerns to address, primarily a weakening margin outlook. In December, I saw the correction not as an obvious green light because of the margin uncertainties and customer concentration risks. I saw those risks as more pertinent because SMCI operates in an AI infrastructure layer that is fiercely competitive, and the business does not have a very strong moat to protect against sharp margin or revenue erosions.

Valuations at that point were compelling from a historical perspective, diminishing the gap with peers like HPE and Dell. However, my analysis showed that they already baked in a rebound in margins from an expected Q2 low (which has now been confirmed).

The most recent move from ~$30-$35, where SMCI sat for the past 2-3 months, does make the valuation even more compelling today. However, despite that, the current setup does not yet justify chasing a Buy. The fundamental picture remains mixed, with revenue momentum intact but margins not yet showing any signs to feel positive about, reinforcing the core uncertainty around earnings quality. At the same time, sentiment and positioning indicators do not point to a capitulation or a crowded trade that could create a strong tactical opportunity either.

While the downside may be increasingly supported at these levels, there is no clear signal (fundamental or technical) that suggests urgency to step in. Even if I set the news aside, the price reaction does not show any obvious valuation-fundamental disconnect that can be capitalized. Valuations are not necessarily looking cheap, and fundamental turnaround is not yet proven.

Reviewing The Fundamental Thesis

At the time of my December thesis, the guidance for the full year in revenue terms was ~$36b, with a significant part of that backed by a strong backlog. The key concern was a significant gross margin cut guided for Q2 FY2026 - down by 300 bps from ~9.5% reported in Q1. This was seen as an offshoot of early-cycle large customer ramps and management guiding for recovery over time.

The uncertainty of where margins eventually land dominates the thesis rather than the guidance for Q2 in itself because small variations in margin assumptions were seen to have a disproportionate impact on valuation, as per my analysis, with implied fair values ranging widely depending on whether margins stabilized closer to 6% or 10%.

Q2 numbers have been mixed, but key margin concerns have not been addressed. On the growth front, SMCI reported a record Q2 revenue of ~$12.7b (~123% YoY growth) driven by continued strength in AI infrastructure demand. The full-year guidance was raised to $40b, although I had seen the earlier guidance as conservative and the recent upgrade as confirming demand strength but not really moving the needle on SMCI's core thesis. Especially given that Q2 gross margins came in slightly lower than the guided ~6.5%. This is not a major miss, but it is important to understand that the positives have come in an area that was not really looking for further support, while margins could have done with a better-than-expected number but didn't.

The overall fundamental thesis can therefore be deemed unchanged at best (minor negatives on margins offsetting the revenue positives). As per management commentary, the gross margin path ahead remains unchanged and as uncertain. That is, margin pressures are currently driven by customer mix, expedite costs, and supply chain constraints. And management reiterated that this represents a near-term trough, with expectations of gradual improvement as product cycles mature and higher-margin offerings such as DCBBS scale.

So Valuations Decide

With no material change in the fundamental thesis, the onus on whether SMCI presents a more compelling Buy case now after correction squarely rests on valuations, news, and sentiment aside. Valuation has compressed sharply even as revenue expectations have moved higher. However, this needs to be viewed alongside a deterioration in the margin profile - and that is the primary deterrent. At ~10x forward earnings and especially at ~0.3x forward sales, the stock may look cheap when seen in light of recent history (2024-25). But forward P/E valuations have been here before, though, even in late 2024 and been somewhere around ~15x a few times in 2025, when the stock was poised strongly. So this is near the lows seen over the past 2-3 years in forward earnings terms, though not by much. In forward sales terms, however, it does comfortably breach lows seen over the same period.

Now, relying on recent historical benchmarks can be misleading in itself. Earlier valuations implicitly assumed a strong margin trajectory that now remains uncertain in both magnitude and timing. As a result, lower multiples today do not necessarily imply mispricing - they may simply reflect a reset in earnings expectations.

There are two ways to actually evaluate valuations today to understand whether they are truly compelling. The first approach is peer benchmarking. Growth comparisons have begun to matter a lot less now, which is why I anchor my comparison on forward P/E multiples over forward P/S (which may indicate expected relative cheapness in a scenario where profitability remains under pressure).

SMCI's forward P/E ratio has compressed from ~17x in December last year to ~10x, now broadly in line with HPE (~10x) and below Dell (~14x). The gap has shrunk even further than where we were in December, when SMCI was still commanding a premium, although a lot lower than 2025 peaks. The relative positioning needs context, though. Both HPE and Dell operate with more stable margins and are not seeing the same extent of margin uncertainties as SMCI. Hence, SMCI's return to valuations closer to the peer pack is not sufficient reason to jump in on compelling valuations yet.

The second approach to gauging valuations for SMCI is to use the scenario-based valuation framework I had used in December. In this framework, I had assumed standard tax effects, interest costs, operating expenses, and shares outstanding for a revenue of ~$36b and calculated implied EPS over a range of gross margin scenarios (6-10%). That analysis had shown how the model and EPS were highly sensitive to gross margin specifics, even within this tight range. At anywhere between ~15x and ~20x P/E valuations, this meant fair value for the stock could land within a wide range of outcomes - from $6 to $58. Below is an updated version of the model with even more optimistic revenue assumptions of $45b (higher than even current 2026 guidance).

Even after a significant revenue assumption increase, the range of fair value outcomes still remains broad, indicating that this is still a margin-dependent investment case rather than scale. The higher end of the spectrum now becomes ~$75, but the lower end only moves from ~$6 to ~$8.5. In other words, the share value in the lower 20s could imply an embedded gross margin of 6-7% already. While that is an improved valuation scenario compared to what we saw in December (when the model indicated scenarios where prices may have embedded up to 9% gross margins), it is not a screaming Buy zone where gross margin expectations are extremely pessimistic. It is looking more like fair value now, especially when revenue expectations are made a lot more aggressive than current guidance. We are in a valuation zone where recovery is not being assumed as much though - so we may be closer to the floor. But this is not discount territory either. Waiting for exact specifics in terms of cost structure - where gross margins, OPEX, etc., settle is supported - an urgent Buy opportunity hiding in plain sight is not obvious.

Close To The Floor, But Not A Buy

I also tried to evaluate whether there is strength in a tactical setup that can be exploited and did not see anything clearly emerging in technical terms either. The current short interest is at ~14%. That is elevated by usual standards but happens to be one of the lowest levels of short buildup in recent quarters, where SMCI has seen short interest in the mid-teens on average. The sentiment is negative, but not crowded. In other words, the recent decline does not appear to be driven by excessive short positioning, nor does it create a strong short squeeze setup at current levels. No unusual sentiment extreme is suggested that can be taken advantage of.

The volume charts also do not show a sustained uptick in volumes after a one-off repricing event following the export control-related news. That puts to rest a strong possibility of continued exits at this stage and supports a near-term floor as the margin story evolves.

What I was expecting is that negative sentiment driven by recent events may have opened up an unfair advantage to exploit in SMCI valuations. That is not obviously indicated at this point - neither through valuation-fundamental asymmetry nor through any tactical technical trend. What we see is a fairer valuation, but not one that is obliged to go up if the news effect subsides. At this point, unless margin evolution sets the tone over the next few quarters, only further, deeper discounts become a point when valuations alone overpower the thesis compellingly. Hence, SMCI remains a Hold.

data.title: QQQY: Double-Digit Yield Doesn't Justify The NAV Erosion
data.description:
Overview

With the rise of covered call ETFs, there are plenty of places for investors to collect a high dividend yield. Defiance Nasdaq 100 Weekly Distribution ETF (QQQY) offers investors an annualized distribution rate of 30%, but I don't believe the risk is worth the income. When I previously covered QQQY, I issued a sell rating due to the rapid rate of NAV erosion. However, it's been more than a year since my last coverage, and even though the share price has rapidly eroded, the fund has still managed to squeeze in a positive total return. Therefore, I wanted to revisit the fund's value proposition, risk profile, and performance expectations.

Looking at the performance over the last twelve months, we can see that QQQY's share price has declined by nearly 23.5%. Interestingly, the fund was not able to provide any capital appreciation while the market indices hit all-time highs throughout 2025. When including all weekly distributions issued, the total return jumps up to 12.7% over the same time frame. Despite the positive total return, I find myself questioning whether or not the rapid level of price erosion is worth the tradeoff. In this environment of elevated market uncertainty and volatility, I believe there are additional downside risks.

Although I am optimistic about the outlook of the technology sector, the fund's inability to capture any upside growth limits the long-term appeal.

Fund Strategy

According to the latest fund overview, QQQY has total net assets of $163.8M. The fund aims to generate income from its option writing strategy against the Nasdaq-100 Index (NDX). The fund is actively managed, so the expense ratio sits at 1.01% of assets. The fund is a bit unique because QQQY implements a synthetic option writing strategy since it doesn't actually own any of the underlying equities that make up the index. This increases the risk profile since the share price can be less stable, but it also results in higher option premiums that can be collected during periods of market volatility.

Unlike other funds that sell options on a monthly basis, QQQY implements daily option writing. This 0DTE (zero-days-to-expiration) approach means that QQQY gets to reset its positioning every morning and allows the fund to capture income more frequently, rather than get caught holding a position through unfavorable market conditions. When looking at the holdings, we can see the strategy outlined by the expiration dates:

  • NDX - 12/18/2026 $1,000.34C (long-term exposure)
  • NDX - 03/25/2026 $24,069.66C (Long Call)
  • NDX - 03/25/2026 $24,002.45C (Short Call)

As we can see, the fund utilizes a daily spread to harvest the immediate volatility of the index. If the index stays below the bottom end of the range, the options expire worthless, and QQQY will keep 100% of the premium. If the index lands between the two strike prices, the fund will lose some of its premium but may still break even. If the fund were to break above the higher end of its option spread, QQQY will gain from its LEAPS but will see losses from its spread.

Performance Insights

After measuring the long-term performance of the fund, it becomes challenging to justify a position in QQQY. For instance, the Nasdaq-100 continues to sell off as investors remain cautious about the growing threat of AI to software businesses. AI has become so good that it now threatens the moat that many of these technology companies once had. This has fueled a rotation out of technology and into other areas of the market. As the index pulls back from its high, QQQY hasn't really offered much protection despite the distributions issued.

For instance, QQQY's total return on a YTD basis sits around a loss of 4.7% despite a high rate of distributions. In comparison, the Nasdaq-100 has declined by about 4.8% over the same time frame. While QQQY's total return is a bit better, I believe the severe rate of capital erosion offsets any potential benefits. For instance, QQQY's share price has eroded by more than 10%, and I suspect this is because the fund is paying out distributions that it cannot support. The unfortunate reality is that the option strategy may not always produce enough option premiums to support the high payouts, so the distributions will have a direct negative impact on the underlying NAV.

The issue is that QQQY isn't structurally aligned to generate enough income during rapid market declines. If the Nasdaq-100 drops sharply, the underlying option spread may hit its max loss before sufficient option premiums can be harvested. Since the fund resets its option spread daily, the losses are basically locked in and prevent the fund from recovering during market rebounds.

This issue is compounded when we consider that the fund will cap the upside growth that is possible while still participating in 100% of the downside movements. This is most relevant during earnings season when it's possible to see the index jump up by more than a few percentage points daily. The growth of QQQY will be capped at the top end of the daily option spread. So if the fund can experience full downside potential but only capped upside growth, this means that QQQY is likely to continue its downward trend over a longer holding period.

So despite the index racing to new all-time highs throughout 2025, QQQY was able to only partially participate in this growth. This is because of the sharp market decline in the beginning of the year, which was a result of President Trump's initial tariff proposal. As we can see below, QQQY was unable to recover from the initial decline as the market hit its bottom in mid-April. If the Nasdaq-100 experienced one of the strongest historical recoveries and QQQY wasn't able to provide any price growth, what are the chances that the fund will perform well under more normal circumstances?

Therefore, I believe that investors seeking income from the Nasdaq-100 would be better off with one of the many alternative choices out there. The difference between funds ultimately comes down to their option writing strategies, including the tenor, holdings, and structure. I believe that two reasonable alternatives are the following:

  1. Roundhill Innov-100 0DTE Covered Call Strat ETF (QDTE): ~21% dividend yield.
  2. NEOS Nasdaq-100 High Income ETF (QQQI): ~14% dividend yield.

As we can see, these two alternatives have a much lower dividend yield than QQQY. However, they both significantly outperform because they implement an OTM (out-of-the-money) option writing strategy. QDTE is similar to QQQY throughout its synthetic approach, but it differs because of the deep-in-the-money calls that it uses to get long-term exposure to the index. Conversely, QQQI implements a more traditional buy-write approach because it directly holds the equities that make up the index. Instead of writing daily options, QQQI typically sets an expiration of a month out, leaving more room to grow upwards.

Dividend Weakness

Another issue that I have with QQQY is the dividend sustainability over time. As the share price and underlying NAV are likely to erode over time, this means that the fund will have fewer assets working on its behalf to produce income. A lower pool of base assets directly translates to lower option premiums received from its strategy. When these issues are compounded over a longer time frame, the distributions will inevitably decline, and this makes it hard to recommend a position in QQQY for income investors.

When looking at the payout history over the last year, we can already see this lower payout trend starting to take place. This sort of death spiral means that the actual distributions hitting your account will trend downward, especially as the fund starts to more heavily rely on return of capital distributions. If we remain in a downtrend for the rest of 2026, this will mean that QQQY is consistently faced with the scenario of paying out more than it produced in income.

When looking at the most recently available section 19(a) notice, we get confirmation that the fund leans more heavily on return of capital distributions. Return of capital distributions can be tax-efficient, and because they aren't classified as income, they aren't taxed as such. Instead, a return of capital distribution will reduce an investor's cost basis and allow taxes to be deferred until the time of sale.

Furthermore, investors should anticipate that there will be some tax consequences from the distributions received. Portions of the payouts can be supported by net investment income, which is classified as ordinary income and will have the highest tax burden for investors. Therefore, a position in QQQY may be best utilized within tax-advantaged accounts for investors that want to minimize their overall tax liability.

Takeaway

In conclusion, I maintain my sell rating on QQQY at this time. In the scenario where a market pullback continues, the fund is likely to continue eroding investor capital. The fund isn't structurally capable of providing any meaningful upside growth, which was demonstrated through 2025's bull market. The fund is not protected against market declines, but the upside growth is capped, which almost guarantees that the fund will continue trending downward over a longer holding period. For this reason, it is difficult to justify a position in QQQY, especially when peers like QDTE and QQQI exist. These peers track the same Nasdaq-100 Index while providing much higher total returns over time.

data.title: ACRES Commercial Realty: Discount To Remain Sticky Given Inflation Expectations
data.description:
ACRES Commercial Realty (ACR) has dipped 21% from its 52-week high to open up a substantial 37% discount to book value as the mREIT maintains a capital return plan that saw management buy back $10 million worth of its common shares during its fiscal 2025 fourth quarter. ACR does not currently pay a dividend, but its aggressive stock buyback program has driven a sustained reduction in its common stock outstanding. This has formed the primary lever of shareholder returns since the mREIT suspended dividends in 2019. ACR's weighted average number of common shares outstanding as of the end of its fourth quarter stood at 7,412,911, a dip of around 6.4% from its year-ago comp against a broader trend of reduction. ACR does not make a good investment for income investors, as it's an mREIT without a dividend yield. The long-term bullish play here is that of a significant discount to book value from a growing portfolio of credit backed by U.S. multifamily properties.

The mREIT's CRE loan portfolio was valued at $1.83 billion as of the end of its fourth quarter, with this position primarily in U.S. multifamily credit at 81.9% of loans held for investment at amortized cost. This was up sequentially from 74.6% in the third quarter and was followed by office properties, which formed 12.7% of loans. Hence, ACR has pushed through a reduction in its office allocation versus the third quarter, when this formed 18.3% of loans. I last covered ACR in December, highlighting the disparity between the book value and common shares. This divergence has since expanded, with ACR's common shares down 20% since then, while its book value is up $0.38 per share sequentially.

ACR's cadence with originations saw $540 million in new loans created during the fourth quarter, offset by $100 million in loan payoffs for around $440 million in net originations. This was a material jump from the third quarter, where $99 million of new originations were entirely offset by $107 million in payoffs. The weighted average spread on newly originated loans was 2.83%, with the mREIT placing its current and expected credit loss ("CECL") reserve at $20.4 million as of the end of the fourth quarter, down from $26.4 million in the third quarter. Investors will likely cheer the gradual shift to purely multifamily loans, as the broad risk factor of these is lower when set against a U.S. national office vacancy rate that stood at 17.6% as of the end of February.

CRE Loan Maturity, The Discount Gap, And Liquidity

The mREIT's CRE fully extended loan maturity profile is just 2.9 years, a relatively medium-term laddering that will allow ACR to see repayments of all its loans by the end of 2028. This allows better management of duration risk, with a front-loaded stream of repayments providing the backdrop for ACR to maintain momentum with origination after a lull in new fundings. This is set against the rapidly eroding expectations that the Fed will cut rates at all this year. The CME FedWatch Tool was previously pricing in two rate cuts of 50 basis points at the start of this year. This has now dropped to zero and will impact the pricing of ACR's two outstanding preferreds. The 8.625% Fixed/Float Series C Preferreds (ACR.PR.C) and the 7.875% Series D Preferreds (ACR.PR.D) could see a dip as investors move to demand a larger spread for holding fixed-income securities in response to higher prime rates.

Critically, it's difficult to see the discount on the commons being able to narrow meaningfully against the current risk-off environment from rising expectations of a downstream inflation. A no-income mREIT is relatively unique, and I don't see healthy demand for ACR common shares in this market. Hence, ACR could catch a bid if it reinstates some part of its dividend while still leaning on share buybacks as the primary tool to return value to shareholders. The share repurchases during the fourth quarter were essentially completed for under 70 cents on the dollar, a discount that was the main driver of the book value per share growth seen during the quarter, as it drove a $0.71 per share growth. ACR's liquidity as of the end of the fourth quarter stood at $108 million, with $84 million comprised of cash and cash equivalents.

Conclusion

The mREIT's focus on multifamily loans, its history of stock buybacks, and the sequential growth in book value per share are attractive. The mREIT has no capacity left on its buyback program, but it can always expand this at any point. I am rating ACR as a "Hold" against the current macro environment.

data.title: Absci: Downgrade To 'Hold' As ABS-101 On Back Burner And Pivot To ABS-201
data.description:
The last time I spoke about Absci Corporation (ABSI), it was in a Seeking Alpha article entitled "Absci: AI-Driven Platform Could Establish Best-In-Class TL1A Drug Targeting Profile." The hope here was that upon the release of data, the company could show a competing half-life when it came to its TL1A candidate ABS-101 in the targeting of patients with irritable bowel syndrome [IBS].

Today, I'm downgrading the stock to a Hold rating for several reasons. The first of which is that while ABS-101 showed an extended half-life versus 1st-generation TL1A candidates, it failed to make a mark in differentiation compared to 2nd-generation ones. Thus, the second reason for the downgrade is because the company has chosen to shift away from further development of ABS-101. The only way it intends to advance this forward is if it can find a partner willing to move it forward in clinical development [likely toward another indication, not IBD, as this landscape is crowded]. The third reason is because the company has chosen to pivot over to the development of prolactin receptor antagonist [anti-PRLR] ABS-201 for the treatment of patients with androgenetic alopecia [AA].

As a matter of fact, in the most recent update, it is noted that it had successfully dosed the first three dose cohorts in the single-ascending dose [SAD] portion of its ongoing phase 1/2a HEADLINE trial using ABS-201 to treat these androgenetic AA patients. While this program advancement appears to be good, there are a few problems here. The first of which is that it is early in the process. What I mean by that is that in the first half of 2026, it only expects to release the initial safety, tolerability, and pharmacokinetic [PK] profile of this study. The true test of whether or not this drug is effective in being able to treat these patients won't happen until proof-of-concept data is released in the 2nd half of 2026 and early 2027, respectively.

In addition, the company needs to obtain positive data from this phase 1/2a study in AA in order to move on to evaluating ABS-201 for the treatment of patients with endometriosis. Should this happen, then a phase 2 study for this candidate targeting endometriosis patients is expected to start in Q4 of 2026, and then the release of proof-of-concept data in the 2nd half of 2027.

The problem here is that privately held Hope Medicine has its own anti-PRLR candidate it is developing, known as HMI-115. The last time it reported data for the androgenetic AA patients was in 2024, and it has since pivoted over to starting a phase 3 study of HMI-115 treating patients with endometriosis. It is not clear why it shifted over to endometriosis, but this is what it has chosen for phase 3 development.

With the inability to differentiate ABS-101 as an anti-TL1A candidate for the treatment of patients with IBD, plus the shift over to anti-PRLR ABS-201 to AA, I believe it is important to downgrade this to a Hold rating. The reason for this rating is the small chance it somehow produces a superior candidate over Hope Medicine's HMI-115 candidate for either AA or endometriosis. Should it fail to have ABS-201 differentiate itself from HMI-115 in some way, then I would change the rating to a Sell.

ABS-201 For The Treatment Of Patients With Androgenetic Alopecia Areata Must Deliver In Huge Way

As I noted above, Absci Corporation failed to have its drug ABS-101 as an anti-TL1A candidate for the targeting of irritable bowel syndrome [IBS] disorders achieve an extended half-life over second-generation ones. With that in mind, plus the competitive landscape in IBD, it has chosen to put this candidate on an internal halt for further clinical development. In essence, it won't advance this candidate with its own cash but instead advance if it can find a partner that is willing to fund it. This is not going to be an easy task because the IBD field is crowded, and thus, unless it is able to find another suitable target indication to establish a partnership, this might not happen, if at all.

The whole point here is that the company has now shifted over to the development of anti-PRLR candidate ABS-201 for the treatment of patients with androgenetic alopecia [AA] in the ongoing phase 1/2a HEADLINE trial. Specifically, Absci Corporation announced that it had completed the first three cohorts of the single-ascending dose [SAD] portion of this clinical trial. Thus far, the drug has been shown to be safe/tolerable. Before going over this clinical program in detail, plus any catalysts to come about from it, I believe that it is first important to go over what this disorder is and what the possible market opportunity for the company could end up being.

Androgenetic Alopecia [AA] is a genetic form of hair loss that occurs in both men and women. The driver that causes the hair loss for these patients is an androgen hormone by the name of dihydrotestosterone [DHT]. This is responsible for the miniaturization of hair follicles. Another name for this is set out for who it affects as well as follows:

  • For men, it could be known as male pattern baldness.
  • For females, it is said to be that of female pattern hair loss.

The global androgenetic alopecia areata market size is projected to reach $7.31 billion by 2034. This is a very large market opportunity indeed. Consider that this is a very large unmet medical need. What makes me say that? Well, consider that in the United States alone there are 80 million Americans that live with androgenetic AA [Absci Corporation Corporate Presentation March 2026, slide #17]. Not only that, but there hasn't been an FDA-approved drug for these patients since 1990. Furthermore, there needs to be improvement for therapies to not only slow down hair loss but also allow for hair regrowth.

Absci believes that ABS-201 might be able to achieve this, but this remains to be seen in clinical testing. The thing is that there are three stages in the cycle of hair growth, with prolactin [PRLR] being responsible for the first stage of Catagen + Apoptosis regression. They are:

  • Catagen + Apoptosis regression [phase 1].
  • Telogen resting phase [phase 2].
  • Anagen - active hair growth and new hair growth [phase 3].

The endgame goal is to use an anti-PRLR drug like ABS-201 to get rid of phases 1 & 2 and only go straight to phase 3 [the anagen hair regrowth and new growth phase].

In order to see whether or not ABS-201 as an anti-PRLR candidate is going to be able to help treat these patients with AA, it has already dosed the first patient in the ongoing phase 1/2a HEADLINE trial. This early-stage, randomized, double-blind, placebo-controlled study is expected to recruit up to a total of 227 patients both with and without AA. The reason why this is important is because this study is actually going to take a long time to complete. Why do I say that? That's because it has been established to be set as two different parts. There is a Single-Ascending Dose [SAD] portion of this study and then a Multiple-Ascending Dose [MAD] portion as well:

  • SAD portion of study - 32 healthy volunteers
  • MAD portion of study - 147 AGA patients.

The reason why it is important to bring this up is because the company is only currently at the SAD portion of this phase 1/2a HEADLINE trial. The good news is that it has given an update that it has already dosed the first three cohorts of this trial. The first finding is that it has been well tolerated to date. To give you an idea, the SAD portion was laid out as follows:

  • Cohort 1 - 8 patients given a 150 mg intravenous [IV] dose of ABS-201
  • Cohort 2 - 8 patients given a 450 mg IV dose of ABS-201
  • Cohort 3 - 8 patients given a 900 mg IV dose of ABS-201
  • Cohort 4 - 8 patients given a 1800 mg IV dose of ABS-201.

The downside goes to what I noted directly above, which is that it is going to take some time to run this clinical study. Consider that Absci believes that it can release initial data from the SAD portion of this phase 1/2a trial in the 1st half of 2026. The problem with this is that this only deals with the healthy volunteers, and it is only to show initial safety, tolerability, and pharmacokinetic [PK] profile data.

The point I'm getting at here is that in order to see if the drug truly has an effect on AA, it needs to release proof-of-concept data from the MAD portion of this study. This is going to be broken down into interim 13-week POC data in the 2nd half of 2026 and then 26-week top-line data in early 2027.

One treatment that boosts the anagen [hair regrowth] phase would be minoxidil. What Absci is trying to do is to be able to enhance what has already been approved. The first item is to offer up an anti-PRLR therapy like ABS-201 with fewer side effects. Secondly, one that is potentially far more durable. The problem with minoxidil is that it has to be taken constantly, as if not taken, hair loss will resume. Lastly, potentially offer better hair regrowth. Thus, ABS-201 must not only do better than currently available products like minoxidil but must be able to compete with HMI-115 as well from Hope Medicine.

The good news is that in terms of showing improvement over minoxidil, it is on the right track when it comes to preclinical testing. In a 21-day hair regrowth model, ABS-201 showed superior efficacy over 5% topical minoxidil [Absci Corporation Corporate Presentation March 2026, slide #22]. Lastly, based on non-human primate [NHP] testing, it is believed that the half-life could allow the company to dose humans once every 8 weeks [Q8W] or once every 12 weeks [Q12W]. However, there is no assurance of this, and it remains to be seen if it actually translates into humans.

I believe that this program has a good shot, but it goes back to what I stated above in the beginning. Despite Hope Medicine seeing some decent phase 2 data for its study, having shown a statistically significant mean non-vellus target area hair count [TAHC] increase of 14 hairs/cm^2 from baseline, it still chose to move over to an extensive endometriosis program. There has been no update on its AA program since that I could find in the press releases of Hope Medicine.

This brings me to the next point, which is that Absci is only to move on to initiating a phase 2 study using ABS-201 to treat patients with endometriosis if and only if it observes positive data from its phase 1/2a HEADLINE trial. This means if it misses the mark on efficacy or runs into safety issues, then it would not only set back the androgenetic AA program but also the advancement of this anti-PRLR candidate for endometriosis as well.

Financials

According to the 10-K SEC Filing, Absci Corporation had cash, cash equivalents, and marketable securities of $144.3 million as of December 31, 2025. The truth is that this company had to raise a lot of cash throughout 2025. As a matter of fact, it tapped into two different ATM agreements. One was an ATM agreement known as a "Prior Sales Agreement" made with Cowen and Company, where the company could offer and sell up to an aggregate of $100 million worth of common shares of stock. For the year ending December 31, 2025, it issued 10,377,752 shares and generated $35.7 million in net proceeds.

Then, it tapped into another ATM transaction in 2025 as well. This was in terms of an at-the-market offering agreement with TD Securities LLC back in August of 2025. With this agreement, the aggregate amount it could issue and sell was also set to be $100 million worth of common stock. For the year ending December 31, 2025, it issued 927,855 shares and generated $3.5 million in net proceeds under this. That's not all; it had also enacted a big public offering raise back on July 28, 2025, with several underwriters. It sold a total of 16,670,000 shares of its common stock to raise net proceeds of $46.7 million after deducting expenses.

Lastly, in January of 2025, it enacted a strategic deal with Advanced Micro Devices (AMD). The beauty of this deal was that AMD injected $20 million as an investment into Absci through a private investment in public equity [PIPE] agreement. The company has plenty of cash on hand, and I don't believe that there is the risk of any near-term dilution at all. What makes me say this? Well, the mere fact that it has given guidance that it believes it has enough cash on hand to fund its operations into the 1st half of 2028.

The company burns roughly $116.5 million of cash per year. This is broken down into $81.4 million in R&D expenses and then $35.1 million in SG&A expenses.

Risks to Business

There are several risks that investors should be aware of before investing in Absci Corporation. The first risk to consider would be in terms of its anti-TL1A candidate not being able to meet the bar in terms of an extended half-life over second-generation candidates. This was a major roadblock, and the company had decided not to pursue the development of ABS-101 for the treatment of IBD on its own. With that being said, it intends to find a partner that is willing to advance this candidate for either IBD or any other indication.

The risk here is that there is no assurance that the company will be able to find a partner that is willing to fund this candidate any further. Especially since it didn't meet the bar in terms of extending the half-life to be better than that of second-generation anti-TL1A candidates. Nor that it will eventually be able to further advance this clinical candidate into additional testing.

The second risk to consider would be in terms of the development of anti-PRLR candidate ABS-201 for the treatment of patients with androgenetic alopecia in the ongoing phase 1/2a HEADLINE study. As I have noted above, the data to be released in the 1st half of 2026 is only set to be that of initial safety, tolerability, and PK profile data from healthy volunteers.

The risk here is that, first and foremost, there is no guarantee that there will be no safety issues found relating to this drug. Secondly, even though Hope Medicine with HMI-115 has been able to show the drug to work well in 12 men, there is no guarantee that ABS-201 is going to have a similar or superior clinical outcome compared to it.

Speaking of which, this is another risk to consider here with ABS-201, which is that not only must this anti-PRLR candidate show to have superior efficacy to drugs like minoxidil, but it must also appear to have a similar or equivalent profile to that of HMI-115. Plus, without achieving positive data from the phase 1/2a HEADLINE study of ABS-201 in targeting AA patients, the company is not going to be able to initiate the potential phase 2 endometriosis program.

The third and final risk to consider would be in terms of the development of a novel AI-designed anti-HER2 antibody for the treatment of patients with cancer. This candidate, known as ABS-501, has been able to show increased or equivalent affinity to Herceptin [trastuzumab] in preclinical models.

The risk here is that this candidate is still in the process of being developed in pre-IND-enabling studies. The point here is that there is no assurance that Absci will be able to achieve positive pre-IND enabling studies in order to move this candidate forward into phase 1 clinical testing. Nor that ABS-501 is going to be effective in being able to treat HER2-expressing solid tumors in phase 1 and beyond.

Conclusion

Absci Corporation had a major problem relating to the development of anti-TL1A candidate ABS-101 for the treatment of patients with IBD. It was able to get its half-life extension technology in place to overcome 1st-generation candidates. However, with so much competition in place [2nd-generation anti-TL1A candidate], it wasn't able to create a differentiated product worthy of continuing development. That's why it had decided not to spend its own cash on further development of ABS-101 for the treatment of these patients. Plus, it believes that it has a better shot at moving forward with its other candidate, ABS-201, for the treatment of patients with AA and endometriosis.

As I have shown above, this biotech has a steep hill to climb. That's because Hope Medicine moved on straight to a phase 3 program for endometriosis, and not much has been shown since releasing initial phase 2 data of HMI-115 for androgenetic AA. Thus, my conclusion for now is to place a Hold rating on this stock.

Why? Perhaps the company can somehow pull off an extended half-life for ABS-201 in the targeting of patients with either AA or endometriosis. Everything for this company now hinges on if it can deliver solid data from the phase 1/2a HEADLINE study targeting androgenetic AA patients. If it doesn't, then that will set back this company even further. At such a point, I might have to reevaluate the rating of ABSI stock as well.

data.title: Thoughts On The Dollar
data.description:
The dollar's resilience since the war began is not a mystery, but it is being misread. The Dollar Index (DXY) has appreciated roughly 1.8% this month alone, following a modest 0.65% gain in February after January's 1.35% slide. Against every G10 currency, the greenback has advanced since hostilities commenced. All but the Canadian dollar and sterling have surrendered more than 1.5%. The move looks like strength. It is, in part. But it is also a story about positioning, leverage, and the jump in US rates.

Unpacking Safe Haven

Two channels are driving the dollar's gains. The first is mechanical: short covering. For months, traders had used the dollar as a funding currency; borrowing cheap in greenbacks to reach for yield in Latin American bonds, for example, and other higher-beta assets. When those risk positions went south, and they invariably do, they were sold, and the funding currency was bought back, which gives the appearance of safe-haven demand.

Another layer of the same dynamic came from foreign equity investors who had been financing the US current account deficit, and many of them with dollar hedges layered on top. As US stocks tumbled, the shares were liquidated and the short-dollar hedges were lifted, or, for some, the drop in equity values left them simply over-hedged, and they trimmed accordingly. Either way, the bid for dollars was structural, not discretionary.

The second channel is more recognizable as genuine safe-haven demand. War compresses risk appetite, and with the United States now the world's largest energy producer, some investors judge the American economy as comparatively insulated from the oil shock radiating out of the Gulf. That is a reasonable, if incomplete, argument. It is also the kind of narrative that tends to have a shorter shelf life than the positioning adjustment does.

US Rates more than Differentials in the First Instance

What is less appreciated is how sharply the rates landscape has shifted and how that is influencing the dollar in ways that go beyond the usual interest rate differential logic. Since the war began at the end of last month, the two-year Treasury yield has risen roughly 53 basis points. The ten-year yield is up about 45 bp. While theory says interest rate differentials are important, in the short run the jump in US rates seems to offer a better explanation of the dollar’s strength. Perhaps when rates spike on supply shocks and inflation fears rather than growth optimism, it changes the dynamics.

And then there is the Federal Reserve. Before the war, fed funds futures had fully priced two cuts this year and assigned roughly 40% odds to a third. By the time the FOMC met last week, some of that easing had been walked back, but the market still expected at least one cut. The statement moved the needle a little. It was Chair Powell's framing in the press conference that did the heavy lifting. He played down the seemingly incongruous projections, which showed the median view having increased the GDP forecast and growth forecasts while keeping the unemployment rate steady and repeating the one rate this year that was seen in December 2025.

Powell was also unusually candid about the Fed's limitations. He acknowledged that missing the inflation target is more the norm than the exception in recent years and that the war poses genuine challenges for both mandates simultaneously.

Higher Prices Bite

Meanwhile, at the gas pump, the political economy is shifting in real time. Retail gasoline prices have risen every single day since the war began — weekends included — adding roughly a dollar per gallon to bring the national average close to $4.00. Grocery prices appear to be following. This is the kind of inflation that is felt viscerally, that does not require an economics degree to understand, and that generates political pressure with a speed that quarterly GDP estimate does not.

Against this backdrop, the Fed's framing of the labor market deserves scrutiny. Powell's characterization of "recent stability" downplayed the loss of 92,000 jobs in February. He acknowledged, correctly, that the administration's immigration policies have weighed on labor force growth. That is the third policy (joining the tariffs and war) that is deterring this Fed and the next chair from being able to deliver the lower rates that the president has incessantly and angrily demanded.

Powell made it clear that he intends to serve as chair until his successor is confirmed by the Senate, which will not go forward until the investigation is over. He also indicated he would not relinquish his governor seat (term runs into 2028) while the investigation is ongoing in any fashion. This is neither unprecedented nor common. He sees it as a defense of institutional independence. Markets should take him at his word.

DXY

Technically, the Dollar Index peaked for the year on March 13 near 100.50. Since then, it has found support just below 99.00, hovering above the 20-day moving average, which now sits a fraction above that level. The index has spent little time above 100 since mid-last year, and the daily momentum indicators are rolling over from overbought territory. That argues for some consolidation. But speculative positioning, though it has shifted dramatically — net long euro positions collapsed from around 150,000 contracts to barely 20,000, and the market has swung to nearly 70,000 contracts net short yen — does not yet look extreme enough to make a compelling case that the adjustment is finished, especially with the fear the war escalates wafting in the air.

The war premium in the dollar is real. So is the rate support. But both are contingent on an escalation path that remains deeply uncertain. The five-day pause announced this week is being viewed skeptically, and rightly so. There still seems to be a risk that the war escalates. The US is moving more troops to the area, and this is spurring speculation that the US could take Kharg Island, a critical export facility for Iranian oil.

Until there is more clarity, the greenback will retain a bid. When clarity comes, the unwind could be swift. We have seen a couple of such responses.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Wall Street Lunch: Huang, Zuckerberg, Ellison Join Trump's Tech Council
data.description:
Listen below or on the go on Apple Podcasts and Spotify

The president could name up to 24 advisors on new tech panel. (0:15) Memory chip stocks fall after Google unveils more efficient AI algorithms. (1:22) BlackRock CEO Larry Fink tells private credit investors to ‘live with it.’ (1:59)

This is an abridged transcript of the podcast:

Out top story so far, President Donald Trump has appointed some of the biggest names in business a new President’s Council of Advisors on Science and Technology.

The appointees include Meta Platforms (META) CEO Mark Zuckerberg, Oracle (ORCL) Executive Chairman Larry Ellison and Nvidia' (NVDA) CEO Jensen Huang.

Advanced Micro Devices (AMD) CEO Lisa Su and Google (GOOG) (GOOGL) co-founder Sergey Brin will also serve.

The White House says the panel will focus on topics related to the opportunities and challenges that emerging technologies present to the American workforce and ensure all Americans thrive in the Golden Age of Innovation.

It will be co-chaired by David Sacks, who has served as White House AI and crypto czar, and Michael Kratsios, another technology advisor.

Thirteen members have been appointed so far and the total is expected to rise to 24.

"We’re delighted to see NVIDIA CEO Jensen Huang appointed to this council and to have the opportunity to advance American leadership in AI," the chipmaker posted. "America’s strength in science and technology depends on a vibrant ecosystem of researchers, developers, and industry working together to drive innovation and global leadership."

Among active stocks, memory stocks are down after Google revealed a set of new algorithms designed to reduce the amount of memory needed to run large language models and vector search engines.

Micron (MU), Western Digital (WDC), Seagate Technology (STX) and Sandisk (SNDK) were all under pressure.

Chewy (CHWY) missed estimates with its fourth-quarter earnings report and set mixed guidance. But shares are sharply higher as investors eyed the confident tone from the company that stressed it is in a position of real strength with expanding margins and strong cash generation.

And Sarepta Therapeutics (SRPT) is surging after the company posted initial data from Phase 1/2 trials for its experimental drugs targeted at two rare forms of muscular dystrophies.

And in other news of note, BlackRock CEO Larry Fink has some blunt advice for investors frustrated by limited redemptions in private credit funds: deal.

“On the front page of the contract… you’re investing in something that has less liquidity,” he told the BBC’s “Big Boss Interview” podcast. “You have to accept the notion that redemptions are gonna be limited to 5% a quarter.”

“Those are the rules. Live with it.”

In addition, he stressed that unlike the Financial Crisis, private credit does not pose a systemic risk to markets, despite recent redemption pressures on some retail funds.

The Financial Crisis in in 2007 “was based on hidden leverage, just gigantic leverage balance sheets,” he said. “This is not a leverage balance sheet problem.”

While some retail investors are seeking to exit, Fink said institutional demand remains strong. “More people are trying to get in,” he said, adding that “we actually had more subscriptions than redemptions” in the HLEND fund.

And the senior leaders of Meta Platforms (META) stand to earn hundreds of millions of dollars if they help the company reach a $9T valuation in the next five years.

The company awarded new stock options to its seven main executives, excluding CEO Zuckerberg, which are exercisable only if the company's stock price surges at least 88% by 2031, according to filing. Senior execs could enjoy the full benefit of Meta's stock options program if the company's share price jumps more than 500% within the provided time frame.

data.title: Virtus AlphaSimplex Managed Futures Strategy Fund Q4 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Market Review

The fourth quarter of 2025 saw a continuation of themes from earlier in the year. Equity markets continued to rally, with the S&P 500® Index reaching new all-time highs in December. This rally was especially prominent in international equities, which outperformed U.S. equities over the quarter.

In the U.S., a September interest rate cut was followed by additional cuts in October and December, but the U.S. Federal Reserve (Fed) was divided on this approach, due to competing pressures from still-elevated inflation and economic data. However, these rate cuts did not affect yields dramatically as they were already priced-in. The U.S. dollar remained relatively stable, while commodities were more volatile. Gold prices continued to reach new highs, but this quarter also saw a dramatic increase in prices for silver, platinum, palladium, and copper. Energy prices, on the other hand, decreased going into year-end.

Performance

The Virtus AlphaSimplex Managed Futures Strategy Fund Class I (ASFYX, Fund) shares returned 4.09% during the fourth quarter of 2025, while the Fund's benchmark, the Credit Suisse Managed Futures Liquid Index, returned 3.85% for the quarter. It is important to note that there are material differences between the Fund and its benchmark. Another index that may be used for performance analysis is the SG Trend Index as it reflects a peer group of diversified, primarily trend-following investment managers. This benchmark returned 4.86% during the fourth quarter of 2025.

The Fund maintained broad diversification across trend horizons and approaches to trend following in the fourth quarter. The Fund saw gains from its long equity positions and commodities (especially precious metals); currencies also provided some small gains. Fixed income detracted from performance.

Equities continued to climb over the quarter, leading to gains from the Fund's long positions, particularly within international developed markets. The largest gains came from Japanese equities, the S&P/TSX 60 (Canada), and the IBEX 35 (Spain).

In commodities, the largest gains came from long positions in precious metals, especially silver and gold. Base metals, particularly copper and aluminum, also provided gains. These gains offset losses from energies and agricultural commodities. Energy losses came primarily from long positions, despite gains from a long position in EU carbon emissions. Agricultural losses were driven by soymeal and soybeans.

Currency gains came from short positions in the Japanese yen (long USD) and from long positions in emerging currencies like the South African rand and Mexican peso (short USD).

In fixed income, long positions in international and U.S. bonds detracted from performance as bond prices fell over the quarter; the strategy did have gains from short positions in Australian bonds.

At the model level, all three approaches contributed positively to performance, with the adaptive and basic multi-trend models outperforming the short-horizon approaches. Within the portion of the portfolio allocated to short-term, high-quality securities (the money market portion), short-term rates at current levels continued to positively impact performance.

Risk Management

The portfolio is managed to an annualized volatility target of 17% or less. 1 We scale portfolio positions as often as daily in an effort to keep total portfolio risk within its target range. As market volatility increases, position sizes are reduced, and as market volatility decreases, position sizes are increased. The Fund's realized volatility during the quarter was 13.52%, higher than the long-term realized volatility for the Fund. We continue to scale the size of the Fund's positions to keep total portfolio risk at or below its target.

Outlook

2026 began with strong upward momentum in global equity markets. In the coming quarter, we anticipate that markets will continue to monitor the balance between growth, inflation, and policy. The macro landscape has become more fragmented across regions in its approach to monetary policy. Going into 2026, it has the potential for both booms and busts, suggesting a wide range of potential outcomes.

For the United States, there is a potential for overstimulation from macro policies like tax cuts, and from the potential for additional rate cuts, which will be affected by the composition and independence of the Fed. There is also a risk, given high valuations and the potential for weakening economic conditions, that rate cuts may provide little support. All the while, geopolitical concerns continue to be front-of-mind for many investors. In addition to the conflicts we have been watching unfold over previous years, the U.S. invasion of Venezuela may impact oil prices and other global policies.

1 The Fund seeks to target a relatively stable level of annualized volatility (as measured by standard deviation), but the targeted volatility is subject to change. There is no guarantee that the target will be achieved and the realized volatility level of the Fund can be higher or lower than its target volatility at any given point in time. Volatility is not an indicator of expected return or a measure of protection against loss.
