data.title: Why I'm Not Waiting Any Longer To Double Down On Gold
data.description:
Is the bull market in gold over?

Is the bull market in gold (GLD, XAUUSD:CUR) finally over? Nah… don't buy into that absurdity if anyone were to tell you that. Thankfully, based on the most recent research and commentaries that I have parsed and looked into carefully, no one is telling me that the bull market is over. Yet, that doesn't mean that market forecasters or prognosticators have not tried looking into their crystal ball to try and make their guesstimates into why gold suffered a surprising selloff in the midst of the Iranian War.

It was only in January that I highlighted that the bullish thesis in gold was only warming up, but the bear market decline since then has threatened to upend that narrative. So we are given another opportunity to take a closer look at why this has happened, and what it portends from this moment on, and also whether it still represents a great buying opportunity for those of us who have yet to get on board the gold glitter.

As for me, I'm seeing a fantastic opportunity, but first, I know we have to go to some of the key reasons that you may have read about on why this bullish gold train formed a top back in January, before dropping into a rare bear market, as we can see from above.

I'm observing that a bullish reversal signal is already underway this week, aligning very nicely with the possible negotiations taking place right now between the U.S. and Iran about putting an end to the current hostilities. Of course, it's still too early for us to confirm whether it's a temporary ceasefire, or something "more permanent," so to speak.

Nevertheless, I always believe that we must pay homage to price action signals, because they represent what investors are telling us right now as they rejected further selling that initially caused gold to collapse into a bear market this month.

From the price action perspective, this is just about the "near-perfect" signal I to add positions to the yellow glittering metal. For those of you who can read tea leaves (price action), I believe you should be able to observe the bullish print after what appears to be a very nice M-shaped signal that typically precedes solid bottoms alongside an up-trending orientation. Another important observation: gold price action also nearly tested the 50-week moving average (blue line), and getting an affirmative reaction from the dip buyers at the current juncture only serves to improve the underlying strength of the signal, in my opinion.

A structurally bullish gold market remains the base case

For those of you who pay close attention to commentary regarding what has caused the recent gold rally to sputter and then drop into a bear market, I urge you to filter the signal from the noise.

Reasons presented include investors unloading after a crazy run in 2025, or retail investors facing liquidity events due to losses in other asset classes, tapping into their gold exposure to cover these needs.

I have also read about countries that are importing oil that may need to use part of their gold reserves to fund the significantly increased energy costs due to the current near shutdown of the Strait of Hormuz.

I’ve also gleaned commentary highlighting that the market is now pricing in a more hawkish Federal Reserve, if the current conflict in the Middle East does not get resolved expeditiously, resulting in possible interest rate hikes, and thus creating headwinds for gold investors, as gold doesn’t pay interest/income on the holdings. In a sense, investors are less willing to hold gold, as they have other asset classes that give them supposedly higher income returns.

Well, let me just say, good try nonetheless. I will now dissect some of these views, and give you my vantage point on why, while they seem intelligently crafted and with good intentions to help us make sense of the dilemma (gold crashing in heightened geopolitical conflicts), they don't quite matter when we consider the structurally bullish narrative in gold in the long run.

Okay, the market now thinks that rate cuts are no longer the base case when we refer to the latest revisions to the federal funds target rate outlook. But it's not like we are expecting the Federal Reserve to raise interest rates like what they did in 2022, right? It's probably just a temporary pause, allowing more time for the central bank to take a look at the inflationary dynamics, which they need to juxtapose against how the unemployment situation is working out, to come to a more balanced view on monetary policy.

However, what do you think of the state of the gold holdings as a ratio of total reserves? It is still going up, isn't it, and we are not even close to historical levels, as the world starts to diversify more of their holdings towards gold.

While we could point out that after the start of the Russia-Ukraine war, the reallocation to gold as a ratio of total reserves surged, the trend had started even before that, as you can glean from the chart. So the question about whether these reallocations can continue to go higher beyond the 20% mark to >30% mark is something that we need to let play out.

Therefore, even though the Fed funds rate has come down significantly from the 5.3% level back in 2023, it has not stopped investors from stepping up their purchases of gold, as central banks around the world seek to diversify from their dollar-based holdings.

U.S. government debt helps the bullish gold narrative

Next, I think we have gotten an insightful look into how the federal government debt will balloon through the 2050s if we don't take decisive actions to curb our spending profligacy at the current state.

At the same time, it also stunned me when I read about the Pentagon's request for an additional $200B to fund the ongoing Iranian War. Those aren't immaterial sums, mind you. And questions about whether the U.S. could continue to fight these conflicts in the Middle East or even other parts of the world, given the current fiscal situation. It really doesn't help matters, and likely spurs investors to reassess whether it makes sense to hedge with gold even more.

So, unless the U.S. benefits from a significant productivity boom in AI (raising GDP growth markedly), helping them to lower the debt and change the trajectory decisively, the market is not likely to bet on it.

Sure, the current AI boom is benefiting the infrastructure builders, enlightening us with the so-called HALO trade, or heavy assets low obsolescence, as it is commonly known. A very nice expression informing us that investing in companies with high capital expenditure benefiting from the AI boom is the current zeitgeist in this unprecedented AI era.

In contrast, if we had put our money in the battered software stocks, with low capital expenditure and supposedly very high gross margins, that may not be sustainable, as the market now wonders whether that terminal value from their long duration cash flows could be whittled down to nothing over time, as they face potentially damaging disruption themes, maybe not imminent yet, but still debilitating over a period of time.

Hence, for investors, unless the federal deficit trajectory changes for the U.S. due to incredible growth in GDP attributed to a formidable AI monetization experience, something must change (lowering debt/spending?). Also, who knows whether the AI boom could also cause widespread AI related unemployment, and curtail consumer spending, hurting the main engine for the U.S. economy?

In this case, can we blame these investors as they navigate these uncharted waters and continue betting on gold as protection against these ambiguities? And with the world becoming an even more dangerous place, with also worried about the U.S. potentially weaponizing its currency to fulfill its own (self-interested) objectives, keeping too much assets in USD may seem too aggressive, and potentially perilous.

It is with this context that I believe why investors are more than willing to come back to support gold right now, after seeing the price action decline into a bear market, which I doubt it will last too long.

So consider taking full advantage while the opportunity is still timely, and while the momentum swing traders are still nursing their wounds, and have not returned yet.

data.title: WW International Is Great For Losing Weight And Losing Money For Shareholders
data.description:
Shareholders of WW International (WW) lost money as the company, the former Weight Watchers International. Inc, slid into bankruptcy last year and continued to see the new equity issued under the Ch.11 reorganization plan decline as the company restructured their business model. For a number of reasons that will be covered in this article, including too much expensive debt, WW stock remains a sell. This is an update to my prior WW articles.

Bankruptcy Reorganization Plan Was Irrational

WW International filed for Ch.11 bankruptcy, which I expected, on May 6, 2025 and exited bankruptcy on June 24. Under the reorganization plan (docket 18), WW shareholders were "gifted" a token recovery of 0.01073733 new WW share for each old WW share held. (Gifting often makes the bankruptcy process faster and less likely to have lawsuits filed by shareholders.) Based on the current WW price of $17.60, this recovery is currently worth about $0.19 per old (WGHTQ) share.

I think, however, that the reorganization plan was completely irrational because it resulted in WW being highly leveraged. First lien claim holders received 91% of the new stock and $465 million term loans with an interest rate of SOFR+6.80 for their $1.62 billion claim. The rate on this term loan was 10.49% as of 12/31/25.

Instead of having $465 million very expensive debt, WW could currently have no long-term debt if the first lien claim holders just received stock. The value of the stock received would, in theory, be worth more by not having the debt because the enterprise value remains the same. Not only are they now highly leveraged, they are paying almost $50 million annually for interest expenses. They could use this cash to expand their business instead of paying debt holders.

Over the last few years I constantly see irrational reorganization plans being confirmed by the courts which years ago "old school" bankruptcy judges would have rejected. For example, Spirit Airlines reorganization plan was irrational, which I covered, and they ended up back in bankruptcy court.

What Happened With Intangible Asset Values?

Their post-bankruptcy balance sheet using "fresh start accounting" is a complete shock. While goodwill declined, which was expected, from $239.593 million at the end of 2024 to $200.135 million at the end of 2025, net other intangible assets increased dramatically to $490.664 million at the end of 2025 from $115.762 million at the end of 2024.

Year-End Balance Sheet 2025 and 2024

This $374.902 million increase in other intangible assets is greater than the $318.071 million year-end 2025 total shareholder equity. Without this increase, shareholder equity would be a deficit of $56.831 million.

Determination of Net Other Intangible Assets 2025

Determination of Net Intangible Assets 2024

The primary reason for the increase was the $320 million for trade name. I am sorry, but I have serious doubts that their trade names are now worth $320 million. According to their 10-K this number is based on:

trade names valued under the relief from royalty income approach, the royalty rate was estimated to be 6.0%. For the developed technology-based intangibles that were valued using the relief from royalty income approach, the royalty rate was estimated to be 4.0% for Behavioral developed technology and 5.0% for Clinical developed technology

If future revenue does not significantly increase over an extended period of time, there could, in my opinion, be non-cash charges on future income statements to reflect a reduction in the carrying values of intangible assets.

Business Model Still Relies on Membership Subscriptions

WW International's business model still is based on membership subscriptions. This membership model, in my opinion, might have been appropriate when Oprah Winfrey was associated with Weight Watchers and there was a sense of belonging to a group for moral support, while trying to reduce weight via strict diets and exercise. Before they were not really selling a specific product - it was more of a service. Now they are selling specific products, including Wegovy which the FDA approved last December, and attempting to add on support services via their membership subscription program.

They are moving away from their traditional Weight Watchers behavioral subscription model, which they expect to drop 26% in 1Q '26 from 1Q '25, to clinical subscriptions, which they expect to increase to 200,000 at the end of 1Q '26. That is a sharp increase from 130,000 at the end of 2025. An aggressive and expensive marketing campaign in 1Q is a major factor behind this growth.

Membership is not cheap. According to their website:

Offer ends 3/31/26 at 11:59 PM PDT. Pay $25/month for your first two months with a 12-month WW Med+ plan purchase. After the first two months, membership continues for $74 per month for the remainder of your 12-month plan. Programs do not include the cost of GLP-1 medications. All medications require eligibility and a prescription. Plan auto-renews for another 12-month period at the same monthly rate at the end of each plan term unless canceled.

The $74/month does not include the actual cost for GLP-1. With recent soaring gasoline prices I can see why WW stock is dropping. Some people on tight budgets can't afford $74/month.

According to CEO Tara Comonte during the recent conference call "we're about so much more than just a prescription". OK, but is the "more" actually worth $74/month? This is what members get according to their website:

  • Meet with a Registered Dietitian for easier, tailored meal planning

  • Use your camera to track meals and their Points® value

  • 350+ ZeroPoint, no-track foods

  • Automatic macro tracking of any food you eat

  • Generate Points® value totals for any recipe on the internet

  • Access to more affordable weight-loss meds

Their potential growth is huge because according to the CDC 41.9% of adults over 20 are obese (obesity is defined as having a body mass index of over 30). I just think that many potential customers would just decide to buy GLP-1. drugs directly from a discount pharmacy and avoid paying $888 for year's membership. Taking GLP-1 is, in theory, the easy way to reduce weight. Why complicate this easy process with some expensive support subscription?

Valuation Based on Guidance Numbers

Management is expecting revenue of $620 - $635 million in 2026 with EBITDA of $105-$115 million.

This implies an enterprise value/EBITDA multiple of 4.18x - 4.58x. (Total long-term debt of $465 million minus cash of $160 million, plus equity capitalization of $176 million= $481 million enterprise value based on WW stock price of $17.60) These may seem like low multiples, but they are not, in my opinion.

First, you can not ignore "I" - interest expenses, which are almost $50 million annually. Second, the company is highly leveraged compared to the quality of balance sheet asset values. Third, since many consumers are being squeezed by higher gas prices, it might be difficult to achieve their EBITDA guidance numbers. Next, there is a high risk that their business model transformation will be less successful than WW bulls expect.

Conclusion

You would think that Weight Watchers should be booming since the number of obese adults has soared over the last few decades to almost 42% of the population. The problem is their membership subscription model - it is not cheap. I have serious doubts they can grow their clinical subscriptions to a level that will generate large profits.

WW International missed a chance in bankruptcy to become free of all long-term debt. Now they are highly leveraged with a weak balance sheet that has inflated intangible assets. At this point I still consider WW stock a sell.
